message:
beads: file 7mqpc and grudr, close qyv8n

Adds two new beads surfaced during verification + audit passes, and closes the
power-aware repair bead that was flipped to in_progress while the fix landed.

New:
  - flywheel_connectors-7mqpc (P1 bug): fcp-bootstrap verification lane fails
    with 5 pre-existing tests on CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-p9-r1
    rch exec -- cargo test -p fcp-bootstrap -p fcp-ratelimit -p fcp-telemetry
    -p fwc. Specific failing tests pinned at file:line in the bead body:
      * genesis.rs:1138 fingerprint_differs_across_non_deterministic_genesis
      * genesis.rs:1257 genesis_deterministic_and_regular_differ_in_time
      * soft_token.rs:934 workflow_bootstrap_mixed_keys_selects_ed25519
      * workflow.rs:1221 test_finalize_bootstrap_preserves_partial_state_marker
        _when_save_genesis_fails
      * workflow.rs:1845 test_check_init_state_corrupt_genesis_with_partial_
        state_prefers_recovery_marker
    Blocks the cross-crate verification lane.

  - flywheel_connectors-grudr (P2 bug, security/test-hygiene): fcp-registry
    exports three trust-bypass verifiers (MockTufVerifier, MockSigstoreVerifier,
    MockTransparencyVerifier) as pub from src/lib.rs with no cfg(test) or
    feature gate. A downstream `use fcp_registry::MockSigstoreVerifier;` would
    get a drop-in Sigstore verifier that accepts any bundle. Grep currently
    shows only tests/no_mock_integration.rs consuming them, so the footgun has
    not fired — the issue is that nothing in the compiler layer prevents it.
    Bead lists three fix options (feature gate, separate testkit crate,
    cfg(test) — NOT recommended because tests/*.rs compiles lib in non-test
    config). Same pattern flagged at fcp-tailscale/src/client.rs:337
    (MockTailscaleClient) — noted inline, separate bead deferred to keep this
    one focused on the registry trust-anchor surface.

Closed:
  - flywheel_connectors-qyv8n (fcp-store: Repair Controller has no power-aware
    deferral despite README claim). Resolved by upstream commit 42b884ab
    (PowerState enum + RepairController::set_power_state +
    battery_defer_threshold_percent config, default 20% matching
    fcp_mesh::device::is_low_battery) and README re-upgrade PLANNED -> LIMITED
    in 99cbe37b. fcp-store suite at 901/901.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-7sv","title":"fcp.spotify: Playback Control","description":"# fcp.spotify: Playback Control (FCP2)

## Goal
Enable safe playback control for user devices:
- play/pause
- skip
- seek
- set volume/shuffle/repeat
- transfer playback between devices

Playback control is a real-world side effect and may require Spotify Premium.

## Scope
- Get current playback state and device list.
- Control playback on a specific device.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `spotify.playback.read`
- `spotify.playback.control` (dangerous-ish)

Policy guidance:
- Some orgs may require approval for playback control in certain zones.

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- For control operations:
  - receipts + audit events include device id + action (no track names)
  - handle “Premium required” as a typed denial (not a generic error)

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `spotify.playback.get_state`
  - `spotify.playback.devices.list`
  - `spotify.playback.play`
  - `spotify.playback.pause`
  - `spotify.playback.next/previous`
  - `spotify.playback.seek`
  - `spotify.playback.set_volume`
  - `spotify.playback.transfer`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - control request formation
  - premium-required error mapping
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Playback actions are safe, auditable, and do not leak listening content in logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-7xm","title":"fcp.kubernetes: Rollout status monitoring and history","description":"# fcp.kubernetes: Rollout status monitoring and history (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide deployment rollout visibility so agents can:
- monitor rollout progress
- detect stalled/failed rollouts
- inspect rollout history
- optionally trigger rollback (dangerous)

## Scope
### Read
- Get rollout status for a deployment.
- List rollout history/revisions.

### Write (dangerous; optional)
- Trigger rollback/undo to a previous revision.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `k8s.rollout.read`
- `k8s.rollout.rollback` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- For rollback:
  - require ApprovalToken when policy dictates (recommended default)
  - emit receipts + audit events with deployment id + revision

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `k8s.rollout.status`
  - `k8s.rollout.history`
  - Optional: `k8s.rollout.rollback`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - status parsing
  - watch-based progress updates (bounded)
  - approval gating for rollback

## Acceptance criteria
- Rollout monitoring is reliable and safe-by-default.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gqvh","title":"[FCP2] Rate Limiting: ThrottleViolation, Backpressure Signals, Quota Enforcement","description":"# [FCP2] Rate Limiting: ThrottleViolation, Backpressure Signals, Quota Enforcement

## Goal
Implement rate limiting at multiple layers: per-connector, per-operation, per-zone, and mesh-wide.

## Why This Matters
Without rate limiting:
- A runaway connector can exhaust external API quotas
- A compromised node can DoS the mesh
- Resource exhaustion becomes undetectable until failure

## Rate Limit Layers

### Layer 1: Connector Manifest Limits
Declared in connector manifest:
```toml
[rate_limits]
requests_per_minute = 60
requests_per_hour = 1000
concurrent_operations = 5
```

### Layer 2: Zone Policy Limits
```
ZonePolicy.rate_limits {
  per_connector_rpm: u32,
  per_zone_rpm: u32,
  burst_allowance: f32,  // multiplier for burst
}
```

### Layer 3: Capability Limits
Capabilities can include rate constraints:
```
CapabilityGrant.constraints.rate_limit {
  max_per_minute: 10,
  max_concurrent: 2,
}
```

### Layer 4: MeshNode Admission Control
Global limits per peer node (covered in admission control bead).

## ThrottleViolation Object
When rate limit is exceeded:
```
ThrottleViolation {
  violation_id: ObjectId,
  timestamp: u64,
  zone_id: ZoneId,
  connector_id: Option<ConnectorId>,
  operation_id: Option<SchemaId>,
  limit_type: LimitType,  // RPM, Concurrent, Burst, Quota
  limit_value: u32,
  current_value: u32,
  retry_after_ms: u64,
}
```

ThrottleViolation is:
- Recorded in audit chain
- Returned in InvokeResponse error
- Triggers backpressure signals

## Backpressure Protocol
When approaching limits:
1. **Warning Zone (80%)**: Emit metric, log warning
2. **Soft Limit (95%)**: Return `retry_after_ms` in responses
3. **Hard Limit (100%)**: Reject with FCP-4290 (RateLimited)

For streaming operations:
- Flow control via FCPC backpressure frames
- Connector can request pause/resume

## Token Bucket Implementation
Use token bucket algorithm with:
- `capacity`: max burst size
- `refill_rate`: tokens per second
- `current_tokens`: available tokens

Tokens are consumed per-operation based on:
- Base cost (1 token)
- Size cost (for data transfer)
- Compute cost (for expensive operations)

## Tests
- Unit tests: token bucket refill and consumption
- Property tests: rate limit never exceeds declared max
- Integration tests: concurrent requests properly limited
- Adversarial tests: burst exhaustion followed by gradual recovery
- E2E tests: ThrottleViolation appears in audit chain

## Acceptance Criteria
- Rate limits are enforced at all layers
- ThrottleViolation objects are recorded for audit
- Backpressure signals enable graceful degradation

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gr1","title":"fcp.hubspot: Pipeline analytics and reporting","description":"# fcp.hubspot: Pipeline analytics and reporting (FCP2)

## Goal
Expose HubSpot reporting surfaces needed for:
- pipeline health dashboards
- stage conversion analysis
- activity/velocity metrics

This is primarily read-only but may include access to sensitive business data.

## Scope
### Read
- Retrieve pipeline definitions (stages, probabilities).
- Fetch metrics:
  - counts by stage
  - conversion rates
  - time-in-stage
  - win/loss trends
- Support time window filters and stable pagination.

### Output shaping
- Prefer aggregate metrics and IDs over raw deal details where possible.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `hubspot.analytics.read`
- `hubspot.pipelines.read`

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Avoid logging raw deal contents.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `hubspot.pipeline.list`
  - `hubspot.pipeline.metrics`
  - `hubspot.pipeline.stage_metrics`
- Consistent metric naming + units.

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - time window handling
  - pagination
  - error mapping

## Acceptance criteria
- Agents can answer “how is the pipeline doing?” without exposing raw CRM PII in logs.

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-grudr","title":"fcp-registry: MockTufVerifier/MockSigstoreVerifier/MockTransparencyVerifier exported publicly from src/ (trust-bypass footgun)","description":"## Finding

Three \"Mock\" verifiers that short-circuit supply-chain trust are declared as \`pub struct\` in \`crates/fcp-registry/src/lib.rs\` with no \`#[cfg(test)]\` or feature gate. They ship in every downstream binary that depends on \`fcp-registry\`:

* \`MockTransparencyVerifier\` — lib.rs:583  (returns \`verified: true\` for any \`entry_hash\` inserted into an in-memory \`HashMap\`)
* \`MockTufVerifier\` — lib.rs:625  (returns \`verified: true\` for any target inserted via \`add_valid_target\`)
* \`MockSigstoreVerifier\` — lib.rs:693  (same pattern — pure in-memory allow-list)

Each implements the real verifier trait (\`TransparencyLogVerifier\`, \`TufVerifier\`, \`SigstoreVerifier\`) so they are drop-in substitutes anywhere the host expects a verifier. A single misconfigured \`RegistryBuilder\` call accepts arbitrary signatures.

Grep confirms they are currently only consumed by \`crates/fcp-registry/tests/no_mock_integration.rs\`, so the footgun has not fired yet. The problem is that nothing in the compiler or lint layer prevents it from firing.

## Why it matters (P2)

1. **Trust-bypass path is one import away.** A downstream developer who writes \`use fcp_registry::MockSigstoreVerifier;\` gets a verifier that accepts any bundle. There is no compile-time error and no runtime warning.

2. **\`impl SigstoreVerifier for MockSigstoreVerifier\` means the mock is interchangeable** with the real verifier in every code path that takes \`Arc<dyn SigstoreVerifier>\`. That is precisely the surface the registry uses to gate signed-artifact installs.

3. **Unrelated bug surface inside the mocks.** Lines 594, 605, 641, 672, 706, 720 all call \`.lock().unwrap()\` on \`std::sync::Mutex\` — a panic on poison, inside async handler code. If the mock is ever plugged into an async code path, a single panic-while-holding poisons the mutex and the next request crashes the handler. Lines 652, 750, 942, 1035 \`expect(\"system clock before year 2262\")\` on \`Utc::now().timestamp()\` — the real panic condition is a \*negative\* timestamp (pre-1970), not year 2262; message is wrong AND the path is unhandled.

## Fix (pick one)

**Option A — feature gate (smallest diff).** Add

    [features]
    test-mocks = []

    [[test]]
    name = \"no_mock_integration\"
    required-features = [\"test-mocks\"]

Then prefix each mock with \`#[cfg(any(test, feature = \"test-mocks\"))]\`. Existing \`cargo test\` callers must pass \`--features test-mocks\` (or the workspace \`[alias]\` in \`.cargo/config.toml\` does it).

**Option B — separate test-support crate.** Move the three mocks to \`fcp-registry-testkit\` (mirroring the \`fcp-testkit\` pattern in this repo). Integration tests \`use fcp_registry_testkit::MockSigstoreVerifier;\`. Zero risk of production imports.

**Option C (NOT recommended) — \`#[cfg(test)]\`.** Breaks integration tests because \`tests/\*.rs\` compiles the lib in non-test configuration.

## Verification

After the fix:

  * \`cargo build -p fcp-registry --release\` MUST NOT expose \`Mock*Verifier\` in the public API. Confirm via \`cargo public-api\`.
  * \`cargo test -p fcp-registry\` MUST still pass (tests access the mocks through whichever gate is chosen).
  * Add a \`#[deny(missing_docs)]\` or a \`/// # Safety: test-only — MUST NOT reach production\` docstring to each type for belt-and-braces.

## Related (same pattern, other crates)

Scan hit exact pattern in \`crates/fcp-tailscale/src/client.rs:337 MockTailscaleClient\` — same public-from-lib.rs shape, same remediation. Separate bead not filed here to keep this one focused on the registry trust-anchor surface, but the fix pattern is identical.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T04:17:58.513580Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T04:17:58.513580Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp-registry","security","test-hygiene"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gu3","title":"fcp.arxiv: Category and topic classification system","description":"# fcp.arxiv: Category and topic classification system (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide structured topic/category classification to support:
- monitors by topic
- clustering and trend analysis
- downstream indexing (CASS/CM)

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Scope
- List arXiv categories and metadata.
- Extract topics/keywords from:
  - title + abstract (minimum)
  - optional full text (when enabled)
- Provide a deterministic `TopicProfile` object:
  - categories
  - keywords
  - confidence

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `arxiv.categories.read`
- `arxiv.topics.extract`

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- If full text is used, it must come from bounded extraction (`flywheel_connectors-ehu`).
- Deterministic output (no hidden model randomness inside connector unless explicitly configured).

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `arxiv.categories.list`
  - `arxiv.paper.topics.extract`
- Canonical output type:
  - `TopicProfile`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Golden fixtures for topic extraction.
- Verify deterministic ordering and bounds.

## Acceptance criteria
- Topic extraction is deterministic, bounded, and useful for monitoring/indexing.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gvama","title":"Connector timeout sweep: extract Duration::from_secs(30) pattern into manifest-driven configuration","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:11:48.658786Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:59.759406Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:25:59.759231Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["config","connectors","hardening"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-gvama","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:04.403273Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1956,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-gvama","author":"jemanuel","text":"EPIC: Connector Timeout Sweep

~80+ connectors hard-code Duration::from_secs(30) in their ConnectorRuntimeConfig construction:
  ConnectorRuntimeConfig::default().with_request_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))

This is the single most repeated hard-coded constant in the entire codebase. While 30s is reasonable for most REST APIs, it's wrong for:
- Long-running operations (Anthropic inference: 300s, video transcription: 600s+)
- Fast operations (key-value lookups: 5s is generous)
- Unreliable networks (need longer timeouts)

CURRENT STATE:
Connectors already use ConnectorRuntimeConfig which has a with_request_timeout() builder. The problem is that the *value* is compiled in rather than loaded from the connector's manifest.toml or an environment variable.

FIX APPROACH:
1. Add a [timeouts] section to manifest.toml schema:
   [timeouts]
   request_timeout_ms = 30000
   connect_timeout_ms = 5000

2. Update ConnectorRuntimeConfig::from_manifest() to load timeout from manifest
3. Support FCP_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS env var as a global override
4. Support per-connector env var: FCP_{CONNECTOR_ID}_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS
5. Hierarchy: explicit code > per-connector env > global env > manifest > compiled default

This is a mechanical sweep that can be done per-connector-family. The manifest.toml schema change is the foundation that unblocks all per-connector work.

NOTE: Not every connector needs individual attention. The pattern change happens once in the SDK/manifest layer, then connectors pick it up automatically. Individual connector beads below are only needed for connectors that need NON-default timeouts (Anthropic, whisper, terraform, etc.).","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:04Z"}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gxx2","title":"fcp.kubernetes connector - Container orchestration integration","description":"# fcp.kubernetes - Container Orchestration Integration Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Deliver the FCP2 Kubernetes connector epic plan for safe cluster introspection and controlled mutations under strict NetworkConstraints, credential handling, and policy-gated dangerous operations.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Strategic Context

### Value Assessment
**Strategic Value Score: 82/100** — Important Tier 3 connector (Infrastructure family).

**Why Critical**: Kubernetes is the **dominant container orchestration platform**, running production workloads at most tech companies. Enables agents to manage deployments, investigate pod issues, scale services, and automate DevOps workflows. Essential for cloud-native operations.

### Priority Matrix Position
- **Category**: Specialized Investment (Infrastructure + High Complexity)
- **Build Phase**: Phase 3 Enterprise (weeks 9-12)

---

## Agent Capabilities (Concrete Use Cases)

### Workload Management
- Deploy and update applications
- Scale deployments
- Rollback deployments
- Restart pods

### Investigation
- Pod logs access
- Event monitoring
- Resource status checks
- Container exec (with restrictions)

### Configuration
- ConfigMap management
- Secret access (controlled)
- Service and ingress inspection
- Namespace operations

### Examples of Agent Workflows
```
"What pods are crashing in production?"
"Scale the API deployment to 5 replicas"
"Show logs from the failing pod"
"Rollback the frontend to previous version"
"What's using the most memory?"
"Check events for this deployment"
"Apply this manifest to staging"
```

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

### Grafana/Datadog Integration
**Full observability stack:**
- K8s metrics via Prometheus
- Pod logs via Loki/Datadog
- Container traces
- Agent correlates infrastructure state with metrics

### Sentry Integration
- Link crashes to pod restarts
- Correlate errors with deployments
- Resource exhaustion → error patterns

### GitHub Integration
**GitOps workflows:**
- Git changes → K8s manifests
- ArgoCD/Flux integration
- Deployment tracking

### Terraform Integration
- Cluster infrastructure via Terraform
- Workloads via Kubernetes connector
- Full infrastructure automation

### Agent Mail Integration
- Incident alerts → agent investigation
- Deployment coordination
- On-call handoff workflows

---

## Technical Implementation Notes

### API Surface
- **Core API** — Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets
- **Apps API** — Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets
- **Batch API** — Jobs, CronJobs
- **Networking API** — Ingress, NetworkPolicies

### Authentication Modes
| Mode | Use Case |
|------|----------|
| Kubeconfig | Local/development |
| ServiceAccount | In-cluster |
| OIDC | Enterprise SSO |
| Cloud Provider | EKS/GKE/AKS auth |

### Recommended Crates
- `kube` — Kubernetes client for Rust
- `k8s-openapi` — Type-safe K8s API bindings
- `tokio` — Async runtime

### Resource Hierarchy
```
Namespace
├── Deployment/StatefulSet/DaemonSet
│   └── ReplicaSet
│       └── Pod
│           └── Container
├── Service
├── ConfigMap
├── Secret
└── Ingress
```

---

## Zone Architecture

### Recommended Zones
| Zone | Description | Capabilities |
|------|-------------|--------------|
| `z:infra:k8s:full` | Full cluster access | All operations |
| `z:infra:k8s:readonly` | Read-only | Queries and logs |
| `z:infra:k8s:namespace:<name>` | Namespace-scoped | Single namespace |
| `z:infra:k8s:deploy` | Deployment only | Scale, rollback |

### Safety Tiers
- **Read**: Logs, status, events — low risk
- **Deploy**: Scale, rollback — moderate risk
- **Write**: Apply manifests — high risk
- **Exec**: Container access — very high risk

---

## Manifest Archetypes (Closed Set)
- `operational` — API operations
- `streaming` — Watch events, log streaming

---

## External Surface

### NetworkConstraints
Varies by deployment:
- In-cluster: `kubernetes.default.svc:443`
- Cloud: EKS/GKE/AKS API endpoints
- Self-hosted: configured API server

### Credential Injection
- Kubeconfig via file reference
- ServiceAccount token via mounted secret
- Cloud credentials via `CredentialId`
- Never write credentials to disk

---

## Capability Model

### Read Operations
- `k8s.pods.read` — List/get pods
- `k8s.pods.logs` — Stream logs
- `k8s.deployments.read` — Deployment status
- `k8s.events.read` — Cluster events
- `k8s.services.read` — Service info
- `k8s.configmaps.read` — Config data
- `k8s.secrets.read` — Secret metadata (not values by default)

### Write Operations
- `k8s.deployments.scale` — Scale replicas
- `k8s.deployments.rollback` — Rollback version
- `k8s.pods.delete` — Restart pods
- `k8s.configmaps.write` — Update configs

### Administrative (Dangerous)
- `k8s.manifests.apply` — Apply arbitrary YAML
- `k8s.pods.exec` — Execute in container
- `k8s.secrets.values` — Read secret values
- `k8s.namespaces.delete` — Delete namespace

---

## Operations (Representative)

### List Pods
```rust
let pods: Api<Pod> = Api::namespaced(client, "production");
let list = pods.list(&ListParams::default()).await?;
```

### Get Pod Logs
```rust
let pods: Api<Pod> = Api::namespaced(client, "production");
let logs = pods.logs("api-pod-abc123", &LogParams {
    container: Some("api".into()),
    tail_lines: Some(100),
    ..Default::default()
}).await?;
```

### Scale Deployment
```rust
let deployments: Api<Deployment> = Api::namespaced(client, "production");
let patch = json!({
    "spec": {
        "replicas": 5
    }
});
deployments.patch_scale("api", &PatchParams::default(), &Patch::Merge(&patch)).await?;
```

### Watch Events
```rust
let events: Api<Event> = Api::all(client);
let watcher = watcher(events, Config::default());
// Stream events as they occur
```

---

## Safety Considerations

### Blast Radius Control
- Prefer namespace-scoped access
- Avoid cluster-admin by default
- Rate limit write operations

### Sensitive Operations
| Operation | Risk | Mitigation |
|-----------|------|------------|
| `exec` | Very High | Disabled by default, audit all uses |
| `delete namespace` | High | Require explicit confirmation |
| `apply manifest` | Medium | Validate against schema |
| `read secrets` | Medium | Mask values, log access |

### Audit Requirements
- All K8s API calls logged
- Secret access specially flagged
- exec sessions recorded

---

## Tests

### Unit Tests (Mock-Only)
- API request construction
- Resource type handling
- Watch event parsing
- Log streaming

### Integration Tests
- k3s or kind for local cluster simulation
- Namespace isolation scenarios
- RBAC enforcement testing

### E2E Compliance
- Validates NetworkConstraints
- RBAC boundaries respected
- Audit logging for all operations

---

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- No real cluster calls in unit tests
- Pod listing and logs functional
- Deployment scale/rollback works
- Event watching operational
- Namespace isolation enforced
- Proper RBAC integration

## Success Criteria
- Connector epic is fully decomposed into implementable beads (manifest/cap map, provisioning, operations, tests, E2E compliance).
- All child beads are implemented and closed, and the connector passes the mechanical compliance runner + its E2E scenarios with structured JSON logs.
- Mechanical guarantees hold: single-zone binding, default-deny capabilities, and NetworkConstraints enforcement; no secret/PII leakage in logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-qvzvp","title":"[MEDIUM] SymbolAck/DecodeStatus replay: signed transcripts lack recipient binding","description":"SymbolAck::transcript_bytes (fcps.rs:748-763) and DecodeStatus::transcript_bytes (fcps.rs:606-634) both include epoch_id (cross-epoch replay protection) but neither binds the recipient NodeId or a per-request nonce.

Within a single epoch, an attacker who observes a valid signed SymbolAck from B addressed to A can replay it to a third recipient D, provided D also has a pending request for the same object_id from B in the same epoch. On D, handle_symbol_ack (node.rs:1416) calls sent_symbols.remove(&ack.object_id), prematurely cancelling D's outbound transfer. Same vector for DecodeStatus via process_decode_status.

Recommended fix: extend SymbolAck and DecodeStatus to carry a `to: NodeId` field (or a per-request nonce echoed from the original SymbolRequest) and include it in the signing transcript. Bump transcript domain separators to V2 to prevent cross-version replay.

Discovered during code review of fcp-mesh after the Apr 19 swarm closed the four-handler signature-verification gap (81f303c8). The signature-verification work itself is correct; this is a residual transcript-binding gap exposed by that hardening.

Severity: MEDIUM. Requires multi-recipient request fan-out in the same epoch to exploit, and the consequence is transfer cancellation (availability), not data exfiltration.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:06:43.709405Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:18:54.488352Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:18:54.488054Z","close_reason":"Bound SymbolAck/DecodeStatus V2 transcripts to recipient node and request nonce","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-r1sl","title":"TEST: Connector SDK Unit Tests (Standard Methods, Typed Schemas, State Model)","description":"# TEST: Connector SDK Unit Tests

## Goal
Validate the connector SDK standard-method surface is correct and ergonomic via a comprehensive test suite (schemas, state, streaming).

## Scope
Comprehensive tests for connector SDK APIs and patterns.

## Test Categories

### Standard Methods
- [ ] invoke() method signature correct
- [ ] simulate() method signature correct (preflight; no side effects)
- [ ] subscribe() method signature correct
- [ ] get_state() method signature correct
- [ ] set_state() method signature correct
- [ ] introspect() returns correct schema

### Simulate / CostEstimate / Availability (Spec-aligned)

#### Core Simulate Tests
- [ ] SimulateRequest/SimulateResponse canonical encoding round-trip
- [ ] `estimate_cost=true` populates `CostEstimate` deterministically when supported
- [ ] `check_availability=true` populates `ResourceAvailability` deterministically when supported
- [ ] missing capability reporting is stable (ordering + content)
- [ ] simulate MUST NOT call write paths in the test connector (mock-enforced)
- [ ] simulate timeouts are enforced and do not leak secrets in error strings/logs

#### Simulate Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_allow.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-001"),
    operation: "email.send",
    input: json\!({"to": "test@example.com", "subject": "Test"}),
    capability_token: Some(valid_token),
    estimate_cost: true,
    check_availability: true,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=true, estimated_cost populated

// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_deny_capability.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-002"),
    operation: "email.send",
    input: json\!({"to": "test@example.com"}),
    capability_token: None,  // Missing
    estimate_cost: false,
    check_availability: false,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=false, missing_capabilities=["email.send"]

// tests/vectors/sdk/simulate_preflight_deny_policy.cbor
SimulateRequest {
    id: ObjectId::from_hex("sim-003"),
    operation: "storage.write",
    input: json\!({"path": "/sensitive/secrets.txt"}),
    capability_token: Some(valid_token),
    estimate_cost: false,
    check_availability: false,
}
// Expected: would_succeed=false, failure_reason="POLICY_DENIED: path_restricted"
```

#### CostEstimate Scenarios
```rust
#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_deterministic() {
    // Given the same inputs, cost estimate must be identical
    let req = SimulateRequest { estimate_cost: true, ... };
    let r1 = connector.simulate(req.clone()).await?;
    let r2 = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    assert_eq\!(r1.estimated_cost, r2.estimated_cost);
}

#[test]
fn test_cost_estimate_no_pricing_tables() {
    // CostEstimate must NOT contain CurrencyCost unless connector
    // has explicit, stable currency basis
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(cost) = resp.estimated_cost {
        // api_credits, estimated_duration_ms, estimated_bytes are OK
        // currency must be None or explicitly stable
        assert\!(cost.currency.is_none() || cost.currency_is_stable);
    }
}

#[test]
fn test_availability_check_rate_limit_headroom() {
    // ResourceAvailability must include rate limit signals when available
    let req = SimulateRequest { check_availability: true, ... };
    let resp = connector.simulate(req).await?;
    if let Some(avail) = resp.availability {
        // Should report remaining/reset if the connector tracks rate limits
        assert\!(avail.rate_limit_remaining.is_some() || avail.no_rate_limit);
    }
}
```

#### Mock Enforcement Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_simulate_never_calls_write() {
    // Mock connector that panics if write paths are called
    let mock = MockConnector::new()
        .on_write(|| panic\!("simulate called write path\!"));
    let req = SimulateRequest { operation: "db.insert", ... };
    // This must NOT panic
    let resp = mock.simulate(req).await?;
    assert\!(\!resp.would_succeed || resp.is_dry_run);
}

#[test]
fn test_simulate_timeout_no_secret_leak() {
    // Ensure timeout errors do not include request data
    let req = SimulateRequest {
        input: json\!({"secret_api_key": "sk-12345"}),
        ...
    };
    let err = connector.simulate_with_timeout(req, Duration::from_millis(1))
        .await
        .unwrap_err();
    assert\!(\!err.to_string().contains("sk-12345"));
    assert\!(\!format\!("{:?}", err).contains("sk-12345"));
}
```

### Typed Schemas
- [ ] Operation schema generation
- [ ] Event schema generation
- [ ] State schema generation
- [ ] Schema validation at runtime
- [ ] Schema evolution rules (V2-only; fail-closed + explicit migration)
  - unknown schema hashes rejected deterministically
  - interface_hash changes are detectable and surfaced via introspection
  - migrations (if any) are explicit and produce new content-addressed objects

### State Model
- [ ] State read/write round-trip
- [ ] State versioning works
- [ ] State conflict detection
- [ ] State snapshot creation
- [ ] State restore from snapshot

#### State Golden Vectors
```rust
// tests/vectors/sdk/state_snapshot_v1.cbor
StateSnapshot {
    connector_id: "test-connector",
    version: 1,
    data: {"cursor": "abc123", "last_sync": 1705000000},
    created_at: "2025-01-15T00:00:00Z",
    checksum: "blake3:..."
}

#[test]
fn test_state_conflict_detection() {
    // Concurrent writes must detect conflict
    let state_v1 = connector.get_state().await?;
    let state_v1_clone = state_v1.clone();
    
    // Writer 1 updates
    connector.set_state(state_v1.with_data(json\!({"a": 1})), version: 1).await?;
    
    // Writer 2 tries to update from same version - must fail
    let err = connector.set_state(state_v1_clone.with_data(json\!({"b": 2})), version: 1)
        .await
        .unwrap_err();
    assert\!(matches\!(err, SdkError::StateConflict { .. }));
}
```

### Error Handling
- [ ] SDK errors use error taxonomy
- [ ] Errors include ai_recovery_hint
- [ ] Panic recovery in connector code
- [ ] Timeout handling

#### Error Golden Vectors

```rust
    "retryable": false
  },
  {

    "message": "Rate limit exceeded",
    "ai_recovery_hint": "Wait {retry_after_ms}ms before retrying",
    "retryable": true,
    "retry_after_ms": 1000
  }
]

#[test]
fn test_panic_recovery() {
    // Connector panics must be caught and converted to errors
    let mock = MockConnector::new()
        .on_invoke(|| panic\!("connector bug\!"));
    let err = mock.invoke(req).await.unwrap_err();
    assert\!(matches\!(err, SdkError::InternalError { .. }));
    assert\!(err.ai_recovery_hint().is_some());
}
```
